episodes.title: Snapchat CEO: Why distribution has become the most important moat | Evan Spiegel
episodes.description:
Evan Spiegel, the co-founder and CEO of Snap, is one of the very few people in the world who has successfully built and scaled a lasting consumer social product. Snapchat has nearly 1 billion MAUs, and Evan and his team invented some of the most important consumer products and features, including Stories, AR glasses, swipe-based navigation, the camera as the primary UX, and a lot more.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why distribution is now the biggest challenge for creating a consumer technology business
2. How Snap innovates at scale with a 9-to-12-person design team: no titles, no hierarchy, hundreds of ideas reviewed weekly with the CEO
3. Why a pure software business is no longer a moat, and what actually creates durable competitive advantages today
4. How AI is changing the way designers work and why they’re now shipping code
5. Why every major Snap feature was copied and how that forced the company to work differently
6. Evan’s prediction that humanity’s comfort with AI will be a bigger bottleneck than the technology itself
7. This year’s crucible moment for Snap
episodes.descriptionCN:
Snap 联合创始人兼首席执行官埃文·斯皮格尔 (Evan Spiegel) 是世界上极少数成功打造并扩展持久消费社交产品的人之一。 Snapchat 拥有近 10 亿月活跃用户，Evan 和他的团队发明了一些最重要的消费产品和功能，包括故事、AR 眼镜、基于滑动的导航、作为主要用户体验的相机等等。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么分销现在是创建消费技术业务的最大挑战
2. Snap 如何通过 9 到 12 人的设计团队进行大规模创新：没有头衔，没有等级��度，每周与首席执行官一起审查数百个想法
3. 为什么纯软件业务不再是护城河，以及当今真正创造持久竞争优势的因素
4. 人工智能如何改变设计师的工作方式以及他们现在交付代码的原因
5. 为什么 Snap 的每一个主要功能都被复制，以及这如何迫使公司以不同的方式运作
6. 埃文预测人类对人工智能的舒适度将是比技术本身更大的瓶颈
7. 今年 Snap ��严峻时刻

episodes.title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
episodes.description:
Cat Wu is Head of Product for Claude Code and Cowork at Anthropic, building one of the most important AI products of this generation. Before joining Anthropic, Cat spent years as an engineer and briefly worked in VC. Today, she’s interviewing hundreds of product managers who are trying to break into AI—and seeing firsthand what separates those who thrive from those who fall behind.
We discuss:
1. How Anthropic’s shipping cadence went from months to weeks to days
2. The emerging skills PMs need to develop right now
3. Why you need to build products that don’t yet fully work, so you’re ready when the next model closes the gap
4. Cat’s most underrated AI skill: asking the model to introspect on its own mistakes
5. Why Claude’s personality is core to its success
6. Why Anthropic’s mission alignment eliminates the friction that slows most large organizations
7. Why “just do things” is the most important principle for working at AI-native companies
episodes.descriptionCN:
Cat Wu 是 Anthropic 的 Claude Code 和 Cowork 的产品主管，构建了这一代最重要的人工智能产品之一。在加入 Anthropic 之前，Cat 担任了多年工程师，并曾短暂从事过风险投资工作。今天，她正在采访数百名试图进军人工智能领域的产品经理，并亲眼目睹是什么将成功者与落后者区分开来。
我们讨论：
1. Anthropic 的发货节奏如何从几个月缩短到几周甚至几天
2. PM 现在需要发展的新兴技能
3. 为什么您需要构建尚未完全发挥作用的产品，以便在下一个模型缩小差距时做好准备
4. Cat最被低估的AI技能：要求模型反思自己的错误
5. 为什么克劳德的个性是其成功的核心
6. 为什么 Anthropic 的使命一致性消除了阻碍大多数大型组织发展的摩擦
7. 为什么“只做事”是人工智能原生公司最重要的工作原则

episodes.title: Why half of product managers are in trouble | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
episodes.description:
Nikhyl Singhal is the founder of The Skip, a community for senior product leaders; a former product exec at Meta, Google, and Credit Karma; and a many-time founder. He’s also one of the most honest, unfiltered voices on what’s actually happening in product management right now.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why the next two years will be the most chaotic period in product management history
2. Why half of current product managers are at risk, and what separates those who’ll do well
3. Why you need to find your “moments of joy” with AI
4. The “smiling exhaustion” he’s seeing across the product community
5. The psychological barriers that prevent people from reinventing themselves
6. Why your resume’s fancy logos matter less than ever, and what matters now
7. His prediction that companies will shed 30,000 people and rehire 8,000—all AI-first
episodes.descriptionCN:
Nikhyl Singhal 是 The Skip 的创始人，The Skip 是一个高级产品领导者社区； Meta、Google 和 Credit Karma 的前产品主管；以及多次创始人。对于目前产品管理中实际发生的情况，他也是最诚实、最未经过滤的声音之一。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么未来两年将是产品管理史上最混乱的时期
2. 为什么现任产品经理有一半面临风险，以及那些表现出色的产品经理的区别
3. 为什么你需要用人工智能来寻找你的“快乐时刻”
4. 他在整个产品社区中看到的“微笑的疲惫”
5.阻碍人们重塑自我的心理障碍
6. 为什么你的简历中花哨的标志比以往任何时候都不再重要，而现在什么才是重要的
7. 他预测公司将裁员 30,000 人并重新雇用 8,000 人——所有人工智能优先

episodes.title: Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
episodes.description:
Keith Rabois was an early executive at PayPal (part of the famous PayPal Mafia), COO at Square, VP of Corporate Development at LinkedIn, and an early investor in Stripe, DoorDash, Airbnb, YouTube, Ramp, and Palantir. Currently he’s managing director at Khosla Ventures. Also, he hasn’t touched a computer since September 2010 (he does everything from an iPad).
In our in-depth conversation, Keith shares:
1. The barrels vs. ammunition hiring framework (and how to spot barrels)
2. Why talking to customers is actively harmful for consumer products
3. How to identify undiscovered talent
4. Why the PM role is dying
5. The three traits of the best-performing companies right now
6. The specific interview question he asks every senior candidate
7. Why CMOs (not engineers) are becoming the #1 consumer of tokens
episodes.descriptionCN:
Keith Rabois 是 PayPal（著名的 PayPal Mafia 的一部分）的早期高管、Square 的首席运营官、LinkedIn 的企业发展副总裁，以及 Stripe、DoorDash、Airbnb、YouTube、Ramp 和 Palantir 的早期投资者。目前，他担任科斯拉风险投资公司 (Khosla Ventures) 的董事总经理。此外，自 2010 年 9 月以来，他就没有碰过电脑（他所有的事情都通过 iPad 进行）。
在我们的深入对话中，基思分享了：
1. 枪管与弹药租赁框架（以及如何发现枪管）
2. 为什么与客户交谈对消费品有害
3. 如何识别未被发现的人才
4. 为什么 PM 角色正在消亡
5. 目前表现最好的公司的三个特征
6. 他向每一位资深候选人提出的具体面试问题
7. 为什么 CMO（而不是工程师）正在成为代币的第一大消费者

episodes.title: Head of Growth (Anthropic): “Claude is growing itself at this point” | Amol Avasare
episodes.description:
Amol Avasare is Head of Growth at Anthropic, which is going through the most unprecedented growth trajectory in history—scaling from $1 billion to over $19 billion in ARR in just 14 months. Previously, Amol worked on the growth teams at Mercury and MasterClass. Before that he was a founder, and he cold emailed his way into the Anthropic role when no job listing existed. Most remarkably, he overcame a traumatic brain injury from a Muay Thai match that meant he couldn't work for nearly a year.
In our in-depth discussion, Amol shares:
1. How Amol landed his role by cold emailing Anthropic’s CPO Mike Krieger
2. How Anthropic is automating growth experiments with Claude (their internal tool called “CASH”)
3. Why the ratio of PMs to engineers might need to flip (more PMs than engineers) as AI makes engineers exponentially more productive
4. Why activation is the single highest-leverage growth problem in AI
5. Why Anthropic indexes 70/30 toward big bets (the opposite of most growth teams)
6. How he uses Cowork to detect team misalignment in Slack
7. How the company’s focus on AI coding created a research flywheel that accelerated their models
episodes.descriptionCN:
Amol Avasare 是 Anthropic 的增长主管，该公司正在经历历史上最前所未有的增长轨迹——ARR 在短短 14 个月内从 10 亿美元扩大到超过 190 亿美元。此前，Amol 曾在 Mercury 和 MasterClass 的增长团队工作。在此之前，他是一名创始人，在没有职位空缺的情况下，他通过冷冰冰的电子邮件进入了人类角色。最引人注目的是，他克服了泰拳比赛造成的脑外伤，这意味着他近一年无法工作。
在我们的深入讨论中，Amol 分享了：
1. Amol 是如何通过向 Anthropic 的 CPO Mike Krieger 发送冷邮件而获得职位的
2. Anthropic 如何与 Claude 一起自动化生长实验（他们的内部工具称为“CASH”）
3. 为什么随着人工智能使工程师的工作效率成倍提高，PM 与工程师的比例可能需要调整（PM 的数量多于工程师）
4. 为什么激活是人工智能中杠杆率最高的单一增长问题
5. 为什么 Anthropic 对大赌注的指数为 70/30（与大多数增长团队相反）
6. 他如何使用 Cowork 来检测 Slack 中的团队失调
7. 该公司对人工智能编码的关注如何创建了一个研究飞轮来加速他们的模型

episodes.title: An AI state of the union: We’ve passed the inflection point, dark factories are coming, and automation timelines | Simon Willison
episodes.description:
Simon Willison is a prolific independent software developer, a blogger, and one of the most visible and trusted voices on the impact AI is having on builders. He co-created Django, the web framework that powers Instagram, Pinterest, and tens of thousands of other websites. He coined the term “prompt injection,” popularized the terms “AI slop” and “agentic engineering,” and has built over 100 open source projects, including Datasette, a data analysis tool used by investigative journalists worldwide. What makes Simon unique is that he’s made the leap from traditional software engineering to AI-native development more fully and visibly than almost anyone—and he’s been documenting everything he learns in real time on his blog, SimonWillison.net.
In our in-depth conversation, Simon shares:
1. Why November 2025 was the inflection point when AI coding agents crossed from “mostly works” to “actually works”
2. How Simon writes 95% of his code from his phone now and why he’s mentally exhausted by 11 a.m.
3. Why mid-career engineers (not juniors) are most at risk right now
4. The three agentic engineering patterns Simon uses daily (red/green TDD, templates, hoarding)
5. The next leap: the “dark factory” pattern where nobody writes or reviews code and AI does its own QA
6. Why prompt injection is an unsolved security problem and the “lethal trifecta” that will likely lead to an AI Challenger disaster
7. Why the pelican riding a bicycle became the unofficial benchmark for AI model quality
episodes.descriptionCN:
Simon Willison 是一位多产的独立软件开发人员、博主，也是人工智能对建筑商影响方面最引人注目、最值得信赖的声音之一。他共同创建了 Django，这是一个为 Instagram、Pinterest 和数以万计的其他网站提供支持的 Web 框架。他创造了“即时注入”一词，推广了“AI slop”和“代理工程”等术语，并构建了 100 多个开源项目，其中包括 Datasette，这是一种全球调查记者使用的数据分析工具。 Simon 的独特之处在于，他比几乎任何人都更全面、更明显地实现了从传统软件工程到 AI 原生开发的飞跃，而且他一直在他的博客 SimonWillison.net 上实���记录他学到的一切。
在我们的深入对话中，西蒙分享了：
1. 为什么 2025 年 11 月是 AI 编码代理从“大部分有效”跨越到“实际有效”的拐点
2. Simon 现在 95% 的代码是如何通过手机编写的，以及为什么他到了上午 11 点就精神疲惫。
3. 为什么处于职业生涯中期的工程师（而不是初级工程师）现在面临的风险最大
4. Simon 日常使用的三种代理工程模式（红/绿 TDD、模板、囤积）
5. 下一个飞跃：“黑暗工厂”模式，没有人编写或审查代码，人工智能自己进行质量检查
6. 为什么即时注入是一个未解决的安全问题以及可能导致 AI Challenger 灾难的“致命三连击”
7. 为什么骑自行车的鹈鹕成为AI模型质量的非官方标杆

episodes.title: The $15B Physical AI Company: Simulation, Autonomy OS, Neural Sim, & 1K Engineers—Applied Intuition
episodes.description:
From building Applied Intuition from YC-era autonomy tooling into a $15B physical AI company, Qasar Younis and Peter Ludwig have spent the last decade living through the full arc of autonomy: from simulation and data infrastructure for robotaxi companies, to operating systems for safety-critical machines, to deploying AI onto cars, trucks, mining equipment, construction vehicles, agriculture, defense systems, and driverless L4 trucks running in Japan today. They join us to explain why “physical AI” is not just LLMs on wheels, why the real bottleneck is no longer model intelligence but deployment onto constrained hardware, and why the future of autonomy may look less like one-off demos and more like Android for every moving machine.
We discuss:
• Applied Intuition’s mission: building physical AI for a safer, more prosperous world, powering cars, trucks, construction and mining equipment, agriculture, defense, and other moving machines
• Why physical AI is different from screen-based AI: learned systems can make mistakes in chat or coding, but safety-critical machines like driverless trucks, autonomous vehicles, and robots need much higher reliability
• The evolution from autonomy tooling to a broad physical AI platform: starting with simulation and data infrastructure for robotaxi companies, then expanding into 30+ products across simulation, operating systems, autonomy, and AI models
• The three core buckets of Applied Intuition’s technology: simulation and RL infrastructure, true operating systems for vehicles and machines, and fundamental AI models for autonomy and world understanding
• Why vehicles need a real AI operating system: real-time control, sensor streaming, latency, memory management, fail-safes, reliable updates, and why “bricking a car” is much worse than bricking an iPad
• How open the platform is: customers can use Applied’s autonomy stack, operating system, developer tools, or mix and match with their own systems
• Coding agents inside Applied Intuition: Cursor, Claude Code, internal adoption leaderboards, and how AI tools are changing engineering workflows even in embedded systems and safety-critical software
• Cruise, Waymo, and public trust: Qasar and Peter discuss why autonomy failures are not just technical issues, how companies interact with regulators, and why Waymo is setting a high bar for the industry
• Simulation vs. reality: why no simulator perfectly represents the real world, how sim-to-real validation works, and why real-world testing will never disappear
• World models for physical AI: hydroplaning, construction equipment, visual cues, cause-and-effect learning, and where world models help versus where they are not enough
• Why robotics demos are not production: the brittle last 1%, humanoid reliability, China’s humanoid marathon, DARPA Grand Challenge-style prize policy, and the advanced engineering gap between research and deployment
• Applied Intuition’s hard-earned lessons: after nearly a decade, Peter says they can look at a robotics demo and predict the next 20 problems the company will hit
• Qasar’s advice to founders: constrain the commercial problem, avoid copying mature-company strategies too early, and remember that compounding technology only matters if you survive long enough to see it compound

episodes.descriptionCN:
从 YC 时代的自主工具将 Applied Intuition 打造为价值 150 亿美元的物理人工智能公司，Qasar Younis 和 Peter Ludwig 在过去的十年里经历了完整的自主弧线：从机器人出租车公司的模拟和数据基础设施，到安全关键机器的操作系统，再到将人工智能部署到汽车、卡车、采矿设备、建筑车辆、农业、国防系统和当今在日本运行的无人驾驶 L4 卡车上。他们与我们一起解释为什么“物理人工智能”不仅仅是车轮上的法学硕士，为什么真正的瓶颈不再是模型智能，而是部署到受限的硬件上，以及为什么自动化的未来看起来不像一次性演示，而更像是每台移动机器的 Android。
我们讨论：
• Applied Intuition 的使命：构建物理人工智能，打造更安全、更繁荣的世界，为汽车、卡车、建筑和采矿设备、农业、国防和其他移动机器提供动力
• 为什么物理人工智能与基于屏幕的人工智能不同：学习系统可能会在聊天或编码中犯错误，但无人驾驶卡车、自动驾驶车辆和机器人等安全关键机器需要更高的可靠性
• 从自主工具到广泛的物理人工智能平台的演变：从机器人出租车公司的模拟和数据基础设施开始，然后扩展到涵盖模拟、操作系统、自主和人工智能模型的 30 多种产品
• Applied Intuition 技术的三个核心部分：模拟和强化学习基础设施、车辆和机器的真正操作系统以及用于自主和世界理解的基本人工智能模型
• 为什么车辆需要真正的人工智能操作系统：实时控制、传感器流、延迟、内存管理、故障保险、可靠的更新，以及为什么“砖砌汽车”比砖砌 iPad 糟糕得多
• 平台的开放程度：客户可以使用应用材料公司的自主堆���、操作系统、开发工具，或与自己的系统混合搭配
• Applied Intuition 内部的编码代理：光标、克劳德代码、内部采用排行榜，以及 AI 工具如何改变工程工作流程，甚至在嵌入式系统和安全关键软件中也是如此
• Cruise、Waymo 和公众信任：Qasar 和 Peter 讨论了为什么自动驾驶失败不仅仅是技术问题、公司如何与监管机构互动，以及为什么 Waymo 为行业设定了高标准
• 模拟与现实：为什么没有模拟器能够完美地代表现实世界、模拟到真实的验证如何工作以及为什么现实世界的测试永远不��消失
• 物理人工智能的世界模型：滑水、建筑设备、视觉提示、因果学习，以及世界模型的帮助与不足
• 为什么机器人演示没有投入生产：脆弱的最后 1%、类人机器人的可靠性、中国的类人机器人马拉松、DARPA 大挑战式的奖励政策以及研究和部署之间的先进工程差距
• Applied Intuition 来之不易的经验教训：近十年后，Peter 表示他们可以通过观看机器人演示来预测公司接下来将遇到的 20 个问题
• Qasar 对创始人的建议：限制商业问题，避免过早复制成熟公司的策略，并记住，复合技术只有在你生存足够长的时间看到复合技术时才重要。

episodes.title: CI/CD Breaks at AI Speed: Tangle, Graphite Stacks, Pro-Model PR Review — Mikhail Parakhin, Shopify
episodes.description:
From running one of the most aggressive internal AI rollouts in tech to building systems that simulate customers, optimize ML pipelines, and rethink search latency from first principles, Mikhail Parakhin is pushing Shopify far beyond “add AI to ecommerce.” In this episode, Shopify’s CTO joins swyx to unpack what actually changed inside the company after the December 2025 model inflection, why nearly everyone at Shopify now uses AI tools daily, and what happens when code generation outpaces the rest of the software delivery pipeline.
We go deep on Shopify’s internal AI stack: Tangle for reproducible ML and data workflows, Tangent for auto-research loops that keep iterating toward better results, SimGym for customer simulation and theme optimization, and Liquid AI for ultra-low-latency and long-context workloads. Mikhail also reflects on PR review in the age of coding agents, the future of CI/CD, Sidekick Pulse, UCP, hiring at Shopify, and the untold story behind Sydney at Microsoft.
We discuss:
• Why Shopify’s AI usage effectively reached company-wide adoption
• The December 2025 inflection when model quality suddenly changed behavior
• Why token budgets matter, but only if paired with strong critique and review loops
• The real bottleneck in AI coding: PR review, CI/CD, and deployment stability
• Why more AI-written code can still mean more bugs in production
• Tangle: Shopify’s reproducible workflow system for ML and data experimentation
• Tangent: auto-research loops for optimizing pipelines, prompts, throughput, and infra
• Why auto-research is democratizing ML experimentation beyond researchers
• SimGym: simulating customers using Shopify’s historical data and browser-based agents
• Why customer simulation is hard to copy without years of real merchant behavior
• HSTU, counterfactuals, and modeling merchants and buyers over time
• UCP and Shopify’s product catalog as runtime infrastructure for agents
• Why Shopify is using Liquid AI for low-latency search and long-context distillation
• Sidekick Pulse, recsys, and where Shopify’s AI platform is heading
• Hiring areas at Shopify across ML, data science, and distributed systems
• The backstory of Sydney, personality shaping, and the early Bing chatbot era
Mikhail Parakhin

episodes.title: 🔬 Training Transformers to solve 95% failure rate of Cancer Trials — Ron Alfa & Daniel Bear, Noetik
episodes.description:
TL;DR: 95% of cancer treatments fail to pass clinical trials, but it may be a matching problem — if we better understood what patients have which tumors which will respond to which treatments, success rates improve dramatically and millions of lives can be saved — with the treatments we ALREADY have!
GSK recently signed a $50M deal for their technology that also includes an (undisclosed) long-term licensing deals for Noetik’s models.  Most big AI plays in BioTech have focused on discovery, and usually result in an in-house development effort (meaning tools companies usually become drug companies). This deal stands out in that it is a software licensing deal, and represents a commitment to a *platform* rather than a *drug*. With attention on other software tools for drug development (see the [Boltz episode]( and Isomorphic for example), it is starting to look like the appetite of Pharma for biotech tools has finally started to grow.  Why the sudden interest?
Timestamps:
(0:00) The challenges of starting a biotech lab and generating data from scratch.
(0:55) Introduction of Ron Alfa and Dan Bear from Noetik.
(4:09) The complexity of cancer: Why "curing cancer" is a misleading concept and the need for new, multimodal data.
(8:24) Identifying therapeutically relevant cancer subtypes to improve clinical trial success rates.
(11:27) The importance of intentional, high-quality data generation in AI biotech.
(17:09) Lessons learned from Recursion Pharmaceuticals regarding batch effects and data design.
(20:14) Introduction to Noetik's core data modalities: Pathology (H&E), spatial transcriptomics, and genomic alterations.
(30:15) The philosophy of self-supervised learning and avoiding bias from electronic health records.
(36:01) Translating latent space embeddings and patient clusters into actionable insights for pharma.
(41:40) Using PerturbMap and in-vivo mouse models to validate human AI predictions.
(53:38) Technical deep dive: The Tario transformer-based model and auto-regressive training objectives.
(1:00:26) The GSK partnership: Licensing OctoVC and the shift toward platform-based biotech deals.
(1:13:55) Advice for small biotech AI startups: Scaling, data conviction, and lessons from scientific history.
episodes.descriptionCN:
TL;DR：95% 的癌症治疗未能通过临床试验，但这可能是一个匹配问题——如果我们更好地了解哪些患者患有哪些肿瘤，哪些会对哪些治疗产生反应，成功率将显着提高，并且可以挽救数百万人的生命——利用我们已有的治疗方法！
GSK 最近签署了一项价值 5000 万美元的技术协议，其中还包括 Noetik 模型的（未公开）长期许可协议。  生物技术领域的大多数大型人工智能游戏都专注于发现，通常会导致内部开发工作（这意味着工具公司通常会成为制药公司）。这笔交易的突出之处在于，它是一项软件许可交易，代表着对“平台”而不是“药物”的承诺。随着人们对其他药物开发软件工具的关注（参见 [Boltz 事件]（以及 Isomorphic 等）），制药公司对生物技术工具的兴趣似乎终于开始增长。为什么会突然产生兴趣？

episodes.title: ⚡️ How to turn Documents into Knowledge: Graphs in Modern AI — Emil Eifrem, CEO Neo4J
episodes.description:
The core argument: AI systems need more than top-K chunks. They need structured context about entities, relationships, permissions, authorship, provenance, and history. GraphRAG combines vector search with graph traversal so retrieval can start semantically, then expand through meaningful relationships. This makes answers more accurate, easier to debug, and more explainable. Emil Eifrem, CEO of Neo4J, explains why graph databases are becoming newly important in AI systems. The conversation covers Neo4j’s origin, GraphRAG, knowledge graphs, agent memory, and why future AI applications may need graph-shaped context layers.

episodes.descriptionCN:
核心论点：人工智能系统需要的不仅仅是前 K 个块。他们需要有关实体、关系、权限、作者身份、出处和历史的结构化上下文。 GraphRAG 将向量搜索与图遍历相结合，因此检索可以从语义上开始，然后通过有意义的关系进行扩展。这使得答案更加准确、更容易调试并且更易于解释。 Neo4J 首席执行官 Emil Eifrem 解释了为什么图数据库在人工智能系统中变得越来越重要。对话涵盖 Neo4j 的起源、GraphRAG、知识图、代理内存��以及为什么未来的人工智能应用程序可能需要图形上下文层。

episodes.title: Notion’s Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work
episodes.description:
Sarah Sachs and Simon Last of Notion join us for a deep dive into how Notion built Custom Agents, why it took years and multiple rebuilds to get right, and what it means to turn a productivity tool into an agent-native system of record for enterprise work. We go inside the product, engineering, evals, pricing, and org design decisions behind one of the most ambitious AI product efforts in software today—from early failed tool-calling experiments in 2022 to agent harnesses, progressive tool disclosure, meeting notes as data capture, and the long-term vision for software factories and agentic work.
We discuss:
• Why early agent attempts failed: no tool-calling standard, short context windows, unreliable models, and too much complexity exposed to the model
• The “Agent Lab” thesis for application companies: not just wrapping a model, but understanding how people collaborate and building the right product system around frontier capabilities
• How Notion thinks about roadmap timing: not swimming upstream against model limitations, but also building early enough that the product is ready when the models are
• Why coding agents feel like the kernel of AGI, and how Notion is thinking about “software factories” made up of agents that spec, code, test, debug, review, and maintain codebases together
• How Sarah runs AI engineering at Notion: objective-setting over idea ownership, low-ego teams comfortable deleting their own work, and a culture designed to swarm around fast-changing opportunities
• How Notion organizes AI: core AI capabilities and infrastructure, product packaging teams, and a broader company mandate that every product surface must increasingly work for both humans and agents
• Notion’s eval philosophy: regression tests, launch-quality evals, and “frontier/headroom” evals that intentionally only pass ~30% of the time so the company can see where model capabilities are going
• What a “Model Behavior Engineer” is, and why Notion treats eval writing, failure analysis, and model understanding as a distinct function rather than just software engineering
• How agents compose inside Notion: shared databases as primitives, agents invoking other agents, “manager agents” supervising dozens of specialized agents, and memory implemented simply as pages and databases
• Notion’s take on MCP vs CLI: why Simon is bullish on CLI’s self-debugging nature, where MCP still makes sense, and how Sarah thinks about capability, determinism, permissioning, and pricing alignment
• The evolution of Notion’s internal agent harness: from early JavaScript coding agents, to custom XML, to Markdown and SQL-like abstractions, to tool definitions, progressive disclosure, and a much shorter system prompt
• How Notion prices Custom Agents: credits as an abstraction over tokens, model type, serving tier, web search, and future sandbox costs; why usage-based pricing was necessary; and how “auto” tries to match the right model to the right task
• Why Notion is not eager to train a foundation model, where they do fine-tune and optimize today, and why retrieval/ranking is one of the most important investment areas as more searches come from agents rather than humans

episodes.descriptionCN:
Notion 的 Sarah Sachs 和 Simon Last 与我们一起深入探讨 Notion 如何构建自定义代理、为什么需要数年时间和多次重建才能正确，以及将生产力工具转变为企业工作的代理本机记录系统意味着什么。我们深入了解当今软件领域最雄心勃勃的人工智能产品努力之一背后的产品、工程、评估、定价和组织设计决策——从 2022 年早期失败的工具调用实验到代理工具、渐进式工具披露、作为数据捕获的会议记录，以及软件工厂和代理工作的长期愿景。
我们讨论：
• 早期代理尝试失败的原因：没有工具调用标准、上下文窗口短、模型不可靠以及模型过于复杂
• 针对应用公司的“代理实验室”论文：不仅仅是包装模型，而是了解人们如何协作并围绕前沿功能构建正确的产品系统
• Notion 如何考虑路线图时间安排：不要逆流而上克服模型限制，而且要尽早构建，以便在模型完成时产品就准备就绪。
• 为什么编码代理感觉像是 AGI 的核心，以及 Notion 如何考虑由代理组成的“软件工厂”，这些代理一起规范、编码、测试、调试、审查和维护代码库
• Sarah 如何在 Notion 运营人工智能工程：目标设定高于创意所有权、自负的团队乐于删除自己的工作，以及围绕快速变化的机会而设计的文化
• Notion 如何组织人工智能：核心人工智能功能和基础设施、产品包装团队以及更广泛的公司要求，即每个产品表面必须越来越多地为人类和代理服务
• Notion 的评估理念：回归测试、发布质量评估和“前沿/余量”评估，有意仅在约 30% 的时间内通过，以便公司能够了解模型功能的发展方向
• 什么是“模型行为工程师”，以及为什么 Notion 将评估编写、故障分析和模型理解视为一种独特的功能，而不仅仅是软件工程
• 代理如何在Notion内部组成：作为原语的共享数据库、调用其他代理的代理、监督数十个专门代理的“管理器代理”以及简单地实现为页面和数据库的内存
• Notion 对 MCP 与 CLI 的看法：为什么 Simon 看好 CLI 的自调试性质、MCP 仍然有意义，以及 Sarah 如何看待功能、决定论、许可和定价一致性
• Notion 内部代理工具的演变：从早期的 JavaScript 编码代理，到自定义 XML，到 Markdown 和类似 SQL 的抽象，再到工具定义、渐进式披露和更短的系统提示符
• Notion 如何为自定义代理定价：信用作为代币、模型类型、服务层、网络搜索和未来沙箱成本的抽象；为什么基于使用的定价是必要的；以及“自动”如何尝试将正确的模型与正确的任务相匹配
• 为什么 Notion 不急于训练基础模型（他们现在正在对基础模型进行微调和优化），以及为什么检索/排名是最重要的投资领域之一，因为越来越多的搜索来自代理而不是人类

episodes.title: Ferrari: What happens when you staple a luxury brand to a sports team? (Audio)
episodes.description:
Ferrari is the pinnacle of luxury scarcity — across its entire 79-year history, the company has sold just 330,000 cars at an average price today of $500,000. For context, Hermes sells that many Birkins and Kellys roughly every 2 years, and Rolex moves that many watches every 3 months.
And yet this ultimate luxury product also lives under the same roof with a widely-beloved professional sports team… one with 400 million rabid fans from all walks of life who live and die by the Scuderia’s performance every F1 race weekend! How is it possible that these two seemingly contradictory customer bases can coexist within the same company? And far from destroying each other’s value, only reinforce it?
The answer, it turns out, is a beautiful, bloody, tragic and romantic opera that spans two families and three generations — and just might be one of the best tales we’ve ever told on Acquired. Buckle up for the story of Ferrari.

episodes.descriptionCN:
法拉利是奢侈品稀缺性的顶峰——在其整个 79 年的历史中，该公司仅售出了 33 万辆汽车，而如今的平均价格为 50 万美元。就上下文而言，爱马仕大约每两年就会售出那么多铂金包和凯莉包，而劳力士每三个月就会售出那么多手表。
然而，这款终极奢华产品还与广受喜爱的专业运动队生活在同一屋檐下……这支队伍拥有来自各行各业的 4 亿狂热粉丝，他们的生死存亡都取决于 Scuderia 每个 F1 比赛周末的表现！这两个看似矛盾的客户群怎么可能在同一家公司中共存呢？而且非但没有破坏彼此的价值，反而强化了它？
事实证明，答案是一部跨越两个家庭、三代人的美丽、血腥、悲剧和浪漫的歌剧，而且可能是我们在《后天》中讲述过的最好的故事之一。系好安全带，聆听法拉利的故事。

episodes.title: All true learning is on the job
episodes.description:
You start with reasoning and then you build up your judgment. And then when your judgment is sufficiently refined, it just becomes taste or intuition or gut feel, and that’s what you operate on. But you have to start from the specific.
If you start from the general, and stay at the level of the general—and just read books of principles and aphorisms and almanacs and so on—you’re going to be like that person that went to university: overeducated, but they’re lost. They try to apply things in the wrong places. What Nassim Taleb calls the intellectual yet idiots, IYIs.
One of the tweets I was going to bring up is exactly that. From June 3rd: “Acquiring knowledge is easy. The hard part is knowing what to apply and when. That’s why all true learning is on the job. Life is lived in the arena.”
Naval: I like that tweet.
Actually, I just wanted to tweet, “Life is lived in the arena” and that was it. I wanted to just drop it right there. But I felt like I had to explain just a little bit more because “The man in the arena” is a famous quote, so I wanted to unpack a little bit from my direction. But this is a realization that I keep having over and over.
episodes.descriptionCN:
你从推理开始，然后建立你的判断。然后，当你的判断足够精确时，它就会变成品味、直觉或直觉，这就是你的操作依据。但必须从具体开始。
如果你从一般性开始，停留在一般性的水平上——只阅读原理、警句和年鉴等书籍——你就会像那个上大学的人一样：受过过度教育，但他们迷失了。他们试图把东西应用到错误的地方。纳西姆·塔勒布 (Nassim Taleb) 称之为“知识分子��白痴”，即“IYIs”。
我要提到的一条推文正是如此。 6 月 3 日起：“获取知识很容易。困难的是知道应用什么以及何时应用。这就是为什么所有真正的学习都在工作中。生活就在舞台上。”
海军：我喜欢那条推文。
事实上，我只是想发推文，“生活就在竞技场上”，仅此而已。我想把它扔在那里。但我觉得我必须多解释一点，因为“竞技场上的人”是一句名言��所以我想从我的方向来解释一下。但这是我不断意识到的。

episodes.title: You only learn by doing
episodes.description:
"Life is lived in the arena. You only learn by doing. And if you’re not doing, then all the learning you’re picking up is too general and too abstract. Then it truly is Hallmark aphorisms. You don’t know what applies where and when.
And a lot of this kind of general principles and advice is not mathematics. Sometimes you’re using the word rich to mean one thing. Other times you’re using it to mean another thing. Same with the word wealth. Same with the word love or happiness. These are overloaded terms. So this is not mathematics.
These are not precise definitions. You can’t form a playbook out of them that you can just follow like a computer. Instead, you have to understand what context to apply them in. So the right way to learn is to actually go do something, and when you’re doing it, you figure something out about how it should be done.
Then you can go and look at something I tweeted or something you read in Deutsch or something you read in Schopenhauer or something you saw online and say, “Oh, that’s what that guy meant. That’s the general principle he’s talking about. And I know to apply it in situations like this, not mechanically, not a hundred percent of the time, but as a helpful heuristic for when I encounter this situation again.”
episodes.descriptionCN:
“生活就是在舞台上度过的。你只能通过实践来学习。如果你不去做，那么你学到的所有知识都太笼统、太抽象。这确实是霍尔马克的格言。你不知道什么适用于何时何地。
很多此类一般原则和建议都不是数学。有时你用“富有”这个词来表达一件事。其他时候你用它来表示另一件事。与财富这个词相同。与爱或幸福这个词相同。这些是超载的术语。所以这不是数学。
这些都不是精确的定义。你不能用它们来形成一个你可以像计算机一样遵循的剧本。相反，你必须了解在什么背景下应用它们。所以正确的学习方法是实际去做某事，当你做的时候，你就会弄清楚应该如何做。
然后你可以去看我发的推文，或者你用德语读到的东西，或者你在叔本华读到的东西，或者你在网上看到的东西，然后说：“哦，这就是那个人的意思。这就是他所说的一般原则。我知道在这种情况下应用它，不是机械地，不是百分百成功，而是作为我再次遇到这种情况时的有用启发。”

episodes.title: Best products are opinionated.
episodes.description:
"Every great founder I’ve seen up close, or even from afar, is highly opinionated and they’re almost dictatorial in how they run things.
Also, early-stage teams are opinionated. And the products they build are opinionated. Opinionated means they have a strong vision for what it should and should not do.
If you don’t have a strong vision of what it should and should not do, then you end up with a giant mess of competing features.
Jack Dorsey has a great phrase: “Limit the number of details and make every detail perfect.” And that’s especially important in consumer products. You have to be extremely opinionated. All the best products in consumer-land get there through simplicity.
You could argue the recent success of ChatGPT and similar AI chatbots is because they’re even simpler than Google.
Google looked like the simplest product you could possibly build. It was just a box. But even that box had limitations in what you could do.
You were trained not to talk to it conversationally. You would enter keywords and you had to be careful with those keywords. You couldn’t just ask a question outright and get a sensible answer. It wouldn’t do proper synonym matching, and then it would spit you back a whole bunch of results. That was complicated. You’d have to sift through and figure out which ones were ads, which ones were real, were they sorted correctly, and then you’d have to click through and read it.
ChatGPT and the chatbot simplified that even further. You just talk to it like a human—use your voice or you type and it gives you back a straight answer.
It might not always be right, but it’s good enough, and it gives you back a straight answer in text or voice or images or whatever you prefer.
So it simplifies what we looked at as the simplest product on the Internet, which was formerly Google, and makes it even simpler. And you just cannot make a product that’s simple enough.
To be simple, you have to be extremely opinionated. You have to remove everything that doesn’t match your opinion of what the product should be doing. You have to meticulously remove every single click, every single extra button, every single setting."
episodes.descriptionCN:
“我近距离甚至远距离见过的每一位伟大的创始人都非常固执己见，他们在经营事情上几乎是独裁的。
此外，早期团队也有自己的看法。他们制造的产品是有主见的。固执己见意味着他们对应该做什么和不应该做什么有强烈的愿景。
如果你对它应该做什么和不应该做什么没有清晰的认识，那么你最终会得到一大堆相互竞争的功能。
杰克·多西（Jack Dorsey）有句名言：“限制细节的数量，让每个细节都变得完美。”这对于消费品尤其重要。你必须非常固执己见。消费领域所有最好的产品都是通过简单而实现的。
你可能会说 ChatGPT 和类似的人工智能聊天机器人最近的成功是因为它们比谷歌更简单。
谷歌看起来是你能构建的最简单的产品。那只是一个盒子。但即使是那个盒子，你能做的事情也有限制。
你被训练不能与它交谈。您将输入关键字，并且必须小心这些关键字。你不能直接提出问题并得到合理的答案。它不会进行正确的同义词匹配，然后它会返回一大堆结果。那很复杂。你必须仔细筛选并找出哪些是广告，哪些是真实的，它们是否排序正确，然后你必须点击并阅读它。
ChatGPT 和聊天机器人进一步简化了这一过程。你只需像人类一样与它交谈——使用你的声音或打字，它就会给你一个直接的答案。
它可能并不总是正确的，但它已经足够好了，它会以文本、语音、图像或任何你喜欢的方式给你一个直接的答案。
因此，它简化了我们所认为的互联网上最简单的产品（以前的 Google），并使其变得更加简单。你就是无法制造出足够简单的产品。
简单来说，你必须非常固执己见。你必须删除所有与你对产品应该做什么的看法不符的东西。你必须小心翼翼地删除每一次点击、每一个额外的按钮、每一个设置。”

episodes.title: Get comfortable with repeated slow failure.
It's not 10,000 hours, it's 10,000 iterations.
episodes.description:
"Malcolm Gladwell popularized 10,000 hours. 10,000 hours is directionally correct, but it’s not exactly correct. It implies that if you spend 10,000 hours doing something, you get mastery. Let’s put aside whether 10,000 is the right number or not.
It’s not just hours put in—it’s iterations.
How many learning loops do you have that drive the learning curve?
What is an iteration? An iteration is when you do something and then you look at the result; you test the result somehow—ideally against a free market, nature, or physics. Then you ask, “Did this work or not? What part of this experiment worked or not?”
And then based on that, you make a new creative guess on how to improve that thing, and you do it again.
The number of times you can do that rotation, that iteration, the faster you’re going to learn. That’s the curve you want to be on.
Great people will distill insights from every iteration.
So it’s not as simple as finding one secret. Yes, every company makes a secret bet. They have a theory as to how the world is going to work out that other people don’t necessarily have en masse, or it’s not conventional wisdom yet.
But along the way, they’re going to discover thousands of insights, and each one will build upon the last, and that’s all going to be driven by the number of iterations they can do."
episodes.descriptionCN:
“马尔科姆·格拉德威尔（Malcolm Gladwell）普及了10,000小时。10,000小时在方向上是正确的，但并不完全正确。这意味着如果你花10,000小时做某件事，你就会精通。让我们先把10,000小时是否正确放在一边。
这不仅仅是投入的时间，而是迭代。
您有多少个学习循环可以推动学习曲线？
什么是迭代？迭代是指你做某事然后查看结果；你以某种方式测试结果——最好是针对自由市场、自然或物理学。然后你问：“这有效吗？这个实验的哪一部分有效或无效？”
然后在此基础上，你对如何改进这件事做出新的创造性猜测，然后再做一次。
你可以进行这种旋转、迭代的次数越多，你学习的速度就越快。这就是您想要的曲线。
伟大的人会从每次迭代中提炼出见解。
所以这并不是发现一个秘密那么简单。是的，每家公司都会进行秘密赌注。他们有一个关于世界将如何发展的理论，而其他人不一定有这种理论，或者这还不是传统智慧。
但一路走来，他们将发现数以千计的见解，每一个见解都将建立在上一个见解的基础上，而这一切都将由他们可以进行的迭代次数驱动。”

episodes.title: Stop using Claude. Start using Codex?
episodes.description:
I sit down with Riley Brown to get a hands-on tour of OpenAI's Codex, which he argues is the most powerful single interface for using AI agents today. Riley walks me through how Codex unifies vibe coding, knowledge work, browser use, computer use, and automations into one app, all running on GPT 5.5. I come in as a complete Codex skeptic who has spent most of my time in Claude Code, and Riley shows me skills, plugins, projects, Remotion, Chronicle, and the in-app browser to make his case. By the end, the question becomes whether the era of separate tools for documents, decks, code, and research is collapsing into a single super app.
00:00 – Intro
03:23 – What is Codex
06:46 – Why a GUI beats the terminal for most users
10:13 – Codex: the all in one platform
12:48 – Atlas browser inside Codex
14:21 – Remotion explained and motion graphics workflows
19:28 – Computer use and Chronicle
22:26 – Plugins, skills, MCPs, and integrations
31:57 – Evals, examples, and good outpu
38:43 – Hard questions: who Codex is built for
40:44 – Browser use plays itself in chess
43:20 – Running Claude Code inside Codex
45:58 – GPT 5.5 cost and effort settings
48:50 – GPT Images 2.0
54:09 – Why most people feel overwhelmed by AI tools
57:09 – Three projects to start with on day one and Closing thoughts
Key Points
* Codex is positioned as a super app where coding, documents, decks, research, and automations live in one interface, with GPT 5.5 as the underlying model.
* The trend across Codex, Cursor, and the Claude Code desktop app is the same GUI pattern: chats on the left, agent in the middle, output on the right.
* Plugins offer official integrations like Slack, Notion, Sheets, Remotion, and Canva, while skills are user-created instructions stored as a SKILL.md file.
* Computer use and browser use have crossed a speed threshold; the chess demo runs at near-human pace, a leap from earlier "dial-up" feeling agents.
* Running Claude Code inside the Codex terminal lets you stack both subscriptions and use each model where it shines.
* The biggest unlock for companies is collecting good examples of finished work so agents can match the bar.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Codex Premise I open with Riley's claim that Codex is the strongest interface for AI agents available today, powered by GPT 5.5, and bundled into any ChatGPT subscription. He frames the episode as a tour of how to do all of your knowledge work and coding inside one platform.
2. One Platform for Code and Knowledge Work Riley shows that Codex creates apps, spreadsheets, charts, Word docs, and PowerPoint decks in the same interface, with exports to tools like Canva. He contrasts this with Claude's split between Cowork and Claude Code, which he finds limiting because each surface has separate permissions.
3. Browser Use, Computer Use, and Chronicle We walk through OpenAI's Atlas browser folding into Codex, the speed jump in computer use (controlling Canva, exporting files, feeding results back), and Chronicle, a screen-watching memory layer released two days before recording. Riley flags privacy as a real consideration while still recommending people learn what it does.
4. Plugins, Skills, and Automations Riley untangles the overlapping vocabulary of plugins, skills, MCPs, and connectors. Plugins are OpenAI-approved integrations like Slack, Notion, Sheets, Expo, Remotion, and Canva. Skills are user-built folders with a SKILL.md file, easy to generate by asking Codex to make one. Automations turn any one-shot workflow into a recurring scheduled task.
5. Models, Costs, and Stacking Subscriptions We cover GPT 5.5's pricing (roughly twice the cost of GPT 5.4 via API, around 20% higher than Opus 4.7), effort settings (low, medium, high, extra high), and the trick of opening the Codex terminal, typing `claude`, and running Claude Code inside Codex on the Anthropic subscription.
6. Day-One Projects and Closing Advice Riley closes with a starter pack: have fun first, build a small game and let browser use play it, run a deep research task into a spreadsheet then a doc and a deck, try a 3D simulation, and pick the most annoying daily task to convert into an automation. The deeper message lands as: tinker, look dumb, and follow the rabbit holes.

episodes.descriptionCN:
我与 Riley Brown 坐下来亲身体验了 OpenAI 的 Codex，他认为这是当今使用 AI 代理的最强大的单一界面。 Riley 向我介绍了 Codex 如何将氛围编码、知识工作、浏览器使用、计算机使用和自动化统一到一个应用程序中，所有这些都在 GPT 5.5 上运行。我是一个彻底的 Codex 怀疑论者，我大部分时间都花在 Claude Code 上，Riley 向我展示了技能、插件、项目、Remotion、Chronicle 和应用内浏览器来证明他的观点。到最后，问题就变成了用于文档、平台、代码和研究的独立工具的时代是否正在崩溃为一个超级应用程序。
00:00 – 介绍
03:23 – 什么是法典
06:46 – 为什么对于大多数用户来说 GUI 胜过终端
10:13 – Codex：一体化平台
12:48 – Codex 内的 Atlas 浏览器
14:21 – 远程解释和动态图形工作流程
19:28 – 计算机使用和纪事
22:26 – 插件、技能、MCP 和集成
31:57 – 评估、示例和良好的输出
38:43 – 难题：Codex 是为谁而建的
40:44 – 浏览器的使用就像下棋一样
43:20 – 在 Codex 中运行 Claude 代码
45:58 – GPT 5.5 成本和工作量设置
48:50 – GPT 图像 2.0
54:09 – 为什么大多数人对人工智能工具感到不知所措
57:09 – 第一天开始的三个项目和结束语
要点
* Codex 定位为一款超级应用程序，其中编码、文档、平台、研究和自动化位于一个界面中，以 GPT 5.5 作为底层模型。
* Codex、Cursor 和 Claude Code 桌面应用程序的趋势是相同的 GUI 模式：左侧聊天，中间代理，右侧输出。
* 插件提供 Slack、Notion、Sheets、Remotion 和 Canva 等官方集成，而技能是用户创建的指令，存储为 SKILL.md 文件。
* 计算机使用和浏览器��用已跨越速度阈值；国际象棋演示以接近人类的速度运行，比早期的“拨号”感觉代理有了飞跃。
* 在 Codex 终端内运行 Claude Code 可以让您堆叠两个订阅并使用每个模型的优点。
* 对公司来说最大的解锁是收集已完成工作的良好示例，以便代理商可以匹配标准。
编号部分摘要
1. Codex 前提 首先，Riley 声称 Codex 是当今最强大的 AI 代理界面，由 GPT 5.5 提供支持，并捆绑到任何 ChatGPT 订阅中。他将这一集描述为如何在一个平台内完成所有知识工作和编码的导览。
2. 一个用于代码和知识工作的平台 Riley 表示，Codex 在同一界面中创建应用程序、电子表格、图表、Word 文档和 PowerPoint 演示文稿，并导出到 Canva 等工具。他将此与 Claude 在 Cowork 和 Claude Code 之间的划分进行了对比，他发现这种划分有限制，因为每个表面都有单独的权限。
3. 浏览器使用、计算机使用和 Chronicle 我们将介绍 OpenAI 的 Atlas 浏览器折叠到 Codex、计算机使用中的速度跳跃（控制 Canva、导出文件、反馈结果）以及 Chronicle（录制前两天发布的屏幕观看内存层）。莱利将隐私标记为一个真正的考虑因素，同时仍然建议人们了解它的作用。
4. 插件、技能和自动化 Riley 理清了插件、技能、MCP 和连接器的重叠词汇。插件是 OpenAI 批准的集成，例如 Slack、Notion、Sheets、Expo、Remotion 和 Canva。技能是用户构建的文件夹，其中包含 SKILL.md 文件，可以通过要求 Codex 制作一个来轻���生成。自动化将任何一次性工作流程转变为重复的计划任务。
5. 模型、成本和堆叠订阅我们介绍了 GPT 5.5 的定价（通过 API 大约是 GPT 5.4 成本的两倍，比 Opus 4.7 高出约 20%）、工作量设置（低、中、高、超高），以及打开 Codex 终端、输入“claude”并在 Anthropic 订阅上在 Codex 中运行 Claude 代码的技巧。
6. 第一天项目和结束建议 Riley 以入门包结束：首先享受乐趣，构建一个小游戏并让浏览器玩它，在电子表格中运行深入研究任务，然后在文档和平台上运行，尝试 3D 模拟，然后选择最烦人的日常任务以转换为自动化。更深层次的信息是：修补，看起来很愚蠢，然后沿着兔子洞走。

episodes.title: Hermes Agent: The New OpenClaw?
episodes.description:
I sit down with Imran Muthuvappa to get a hands-on walkthrough of Hermes Agent, a personal AI agent that ships with built-in memory, 40+ tools, and pre-installed skills out of the box. Imran walks me through why he migrated from OpenClaw, how to install Hermes on a Mac or even an Android phone via Termux, and how he cut his token spend by roughly 90% using OpenRouter. We get into agent design (one agent vs. multiple), connecting Hermes to Telegram and Obsidian, and the kinds of prompts that turn a personal agent into a daily operating system. By the end, I have a practical roadmap to install Hermes, pick a model, and start automating real parts of my life and business
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
01:38 – Why Imran Left OpenClaw (Memory, Gateway, Tokens)
04:26 – Hermes Setup Tour and 40+ Built-In Tools
07:06 – Installing Hermes on Mac, Linux, and WSL
12:21 – Telegram and Android Agents
17:09 – Auditing Your Life With Your Agent
20:04 – Must-Know Hermes Tips: Updates, Tailscale, Telegram
21:07 – Should You Migrate From OpenClaw?
25:58 – Hermes + Obsidian as a Daily Dashboard
27:16 – Must-Use Prompts for a Personal Agent
31:29 – Must-Install Skills: Obsidian, Honcho Memory, G-Stack
33:04 – What G-Stack Is and Why It Matters
34:18 – Customization Is a Trap; Output Is the Skill
35:19 – Closing Thoughts
Key Points
* Hermes Agent solves OpenClaw's three biggest pain points: built-in memory (writes to SQLite on successful tasks), gateway stability, and token visibility.
* Installation is a single command on Mac, Linux, or WSL, and Hermes ships with 40+ tools and popular skills (Apple Notes, Reminders, iMessage, Find My) pre-installed.
* Switching to Hermes with OpenRouter can cut token spend by roughly 90%, from about $130 per five days to around $10 per five days in Imran's case.
* You can run Hermes on a cheap Android phone via Termux + Termux API, unlocking SMS, sensors, and on-device social posting as a cheap alternative to a Mac Mini.
* The real skill is defaulting to your agent for work, then meta-prompting it nightly: "What am I procrastinating? What should I automate? What tool can you build me tonight?"
* Imran recommends pairing Hermes with Obsidian for a clean daily dashboard and installing G-Stack (a Y Combinator-style startup skill from Gary Tan) if you are building a product.
Number Section Summaries
1. Why OpenClaw Falls Short Imran opens with the three problems that pushed him off OpenClaw: no memory (repeating the same instructions), gateway restarts as often as once an hour, and zero visibility into token spend. Hermes fixes each directly and has stayed stable for him for over a week at a time.
2. What You Get Out of the Box Hermes ships with 40+ built-in tools (browser, web search, cron, image generation, home assistant) and pre-installed skills for Mac users (Apple Notes, Reminders, Find My, iMessage). You skip the skills-hub scavenger hunt and start working immediately.
3. Installation and Model Selection On Mac, Linux, or WSL you run one command; Mac users may need Xcode Developer Tools first. `hermes model` lets you pick providers, and Imran recommends OpenRouter for transparent pricing, free models like NVIDIA's NemoTron, and access to Anthropic models at a glance.
4. Hermes on Android and the Muppets Fleet Imran runs multiple agents named after Muppets, including "Cookie Monster" on a Solana Seeker phone via Termux. The Termux API exposes the camera, SMS, Wi-Fi, brightness, and vibration, making an Android phone a cheap, always-on, SIM-enabled agent device for things like on-device social posting and SMS 2FA automation.
5. Designing Your Agent Stack One agent is usually enough; two makes sense if you want to keep work and personal separate. Cron jobs vs. sub-agents is still an open question, but sub-agents let you assign cheaper models to deterministic tasks. Pairing Hermes with Obsidian (Markdown files the agent organizes for you) gives you a readable daily dashboard on phone and desktop.
6. Skills, Prompts: Obsidian, Honcho Dev Memory, and G-Stack (Gary Tan's YC-style startup skill). The prompts that compound: "What have I been procrastinating?" "What is the most important thing today?" "What task should I automate?" "What tool can you build me tonight?" The real skill is defaulting to the agent for daily work; customization is the rabbit hole to avoid.

episodes.title: Claude Design: Full Walkthrough. I'm blown away.
episodes.description:
I go live and get my hands dirty with Claude Design, Anthropic's new design tool in research preview. Across roughly an hour, I run a real workflow end-to-end: pulling a product idea from Idea Browser, generating wireframes, iterating into visual designs, building a pitch deck, and attempting a 30-second video ad. I share my first reactions in real time and take feedback from the chat. By the end, I land on a clear verdict — best-in-class for wireframes and visuals, weaker for video — and give you a practical sense of where this tool fits in your workflow.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
00:50 – Claude Design walkthrough
03:48 – Picking a product idea from Idea Browser: Senior Brains
05:54 – Wire-framing Senior Brains
13:54 – Reacting to the generated wireframe
20:44 – Building a pitch deck for Senior Brains
30:39 – Reacting to a pitch deck
34:58 – Reacting to Hi-Fi wireframe
40:40 – Creating a 30-second animated video ad
48:58 – Reacting to animated video ad
58:10 – Final verdict and recommendations
Key Points
* Claude Design's wireframing is the strongest capability I've seen in a design tool to date, especially the questionnaire that extrapolates like a product manager.
* The pitch deck generation nails roughly 90% of the output with minimal input, which saves hours.
* Visual design mockups come through clean and usable, ready to iterate with 30 minutes of back-and-forth.
* Video generation lands at about a 5/10 — workable as a social post, weaker as a real commercial.
* Start with wireframes first to conserve tokens and sharpen feature decisions before committing to high fidelity.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. Why I'm Going Live With Claude Design — I open by explaining that YouTube tutorials on Claude Design felt inauthentic, so I want to run a real, unscripted session where you watch me succeed and stumble together. The only way to know a tool is to get your hands dirty.
2. Touring The Landing Page — Claude Design opens up with options for prototypes, slide decks, templates, timeline-based motion design, and design systems. I flag the design system feature as something I want to test later — specifically whether I can import a Figma system or recreate one from a reference like Apple.
3. Picking An Idea Worth Building — I grab a real idea from ideabrowser.com: a gamified brain exercise app for seniors with adaptive difficulty. I name it Senior Brains and use a screenshot of the idea brief as my starting context, so Claude has grounded material to work from.
4. The Wireframe Questionnaire — Before generating anything, Claude Design asks a series of sharp questions: primary device, mascot tone, which screens to mock, how many directions to explore, gamification elements, accessibility baselines, and how prominent the family caregiver should be in the app. I call out the caregiver question specifically — it shows Claude is thinking like a product manager, not a design tool.
5. Wireframes, Low-Fi By Design — I pick lowest fidelity because mid-fi wireframes tend to waste time. Claude generates three distinct directions for phone wireframes, and the conceptual range across directions is legitimately useful for picking a path forward.
6. Visual Designs And The Deck — The jump from wireframes into visual mockups produces strong results, and the auto-generated pitch deck for Senior Brains impresses me the most in the session. The deck alone represents thousands of dollars of value if you priced the equivalent work from a designer.
7. The 30-Second Video Attempt — I ask Claude to generate a 30-second animated ad featuring Ruth (a mom) and Sarah (her daughter) connecting through the app. The first version reads more like a social-feed clip than a TV commercial. I ask for a more cinematic version, and it improves but still falls short of what I had in mind.

2. 浏览登陆页面 — Claude Design 打开了原型、幻灯片、模板、基于时间线的运动设计和设计系统的选项。我将设计系统功能标记为我想稍后测试的东西 - 特别是我是否可以导入 Figma 系统或从 Apple 等参考文献中重新创建一个系统。
3. 选择一个值得构建的想法——我从 ideabrowser.com 获得了一个真正的想法：一款游戏化的大脑锻炼应用程序，适合有适应性困难的老年人。我将其命名为“高级大脑”，并使用创意简报的屏幕截图作为我的起始背景，这样克劳德就有了可供参考的基础材料。
4. 线框问卷——在生成任何内容之前，Claude Design 会提出一系列尖锐的问题：主要设备、吉祥物色调、要模拟的屏幕、要探索的方向、游戏化元素、可访问性基线以及家庭护理人员在应用程序中的重要性。我特别提到了护理人员的问题——这表明克劳德像产品经理一样思考，而不是设计工具。
5. 线框，低保真度设计——我选择最低保真度，因为中保真线框往往会浪费时间。克劳德为电话线框生成了三个不同的方向，并且跨方向的概念范围对于选择前进的路径非常有用。
6. 视觉设计和演示文稿——从线框到视觉模型的跳跃产生了很好的结果，而自动生成的高级大脑的演示文稿在会议中给我留下了最深刻的印象。如果你为设计师的同等作品定价，光是这套牌就代表了数千美元的价值。
7. 30 秒视频尝试 — 我要求 Claude 制作一个 30 秒的动画广告，其中露丝（一位妈妈）和莎拉（她的女儿）通过应用程序进行交流。第一个版本读起来更像是社交信息片段，而不是电视广告。我要求一个更加电影化的版本，它有所改进，但仍然达不到我的想法。

episodes.title: 5 tips for OpenClaw
episodes.description:
I sit down with Moritz Kremb, an OpenClaw power user and agency builder based in Berlin, to break down how to actually make OpenClaw useful. Moritz walks through a 10-step optimization guide covering everything from troubleshooting and memory management to model selection and security basics. He then demos two real systems he built with OpenClaw: a full short-form video content pipeline and a conversational CRM. This episode is for anyone who tried OpenClaw, hit a wall, and wants a clear path to turning it into a superhuman digital employee.
Key Points
* Upload the OpenClaw documentation into a Claude project to create a dedicated troubleshooting baseline — it solves roughly 99% of setup issues.
* Use the OAuth method (your existing $20 ChatGPT or Anthropic subscription) to avoid expensive API costs, and always configure backup models.
* Memory problems are almost always caused by memory never being saved in the first place; add an auto-save instruction to the heartbeat file so it logs every 30 minutes.
* Organize your OpenClaw conversations into separate Telegram groups and topics with group-specific system prompts to avoid context bleed.
* Stronger models are meaningfully more resistant to prompt injection; pair that with least-access principles and agent-owned accounts for a solid security posture.
* Custom skills are the path to real automation — whenever you do something repeatedly, tell your OpenClaw to turn it into a skill.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. OpenClaw Explained: What It Is and How It Differs
Moritz breaks down OpenClaw as the first truly personal, autonomous agent — one that remembers, runs on a heartbeat every 30 minutes, executes cron jobs, and lives inside whatever chat app you already use. He maps the evolution from ChatGPT (cloud chat) to Claude Code (local files) to OpenClaw (persistent, proactive, and portable).
2. OpenClaw vs. Claude Cowork and Dispatch
Anthropic's new Dispatch feature and Claude Cowork are moving toward what OpenClaw already does, but OpenClaw remains more powerful and feature-rich today. Moritz frames the long-term comparison as Linux vs. Windows: the open-source version attracts community contribution, flexibility, and customization that proprietary tools have a harder time matching.
3. The 10-Step Optimized Setup
The core of the episode. Moritz walks step by step through: creating a troubleshooting project with the official docs, personalizing workspace files (agents.md, soul.md, user.md, identity.md), fixing memory with compaction settings and heartbeat auto-save, selecting models via OAuth, organizing Telegram groups with system prompts, understanding the three browser modes, activating and building skills, tuning the heartbeat file, hardening security, and applying least-access principles with agent-owned accounts.
4. Model Selection and the OAuth Shortcut
Instead of paying per-API-call, Moritz recommends hooking OpenClaw into an existing $20 ChatGPT subscription via OAuth. episodes.title: How AI agents & Claude skills work (Clearly Explained)
episodes.description:
I sit down with Ras Mic to break down how AI agents actually work and why most people are using them wrong. Ras Mic explains the mechanics of context windows, makes the case that agent md files are largely unnecessary, and shares his step-by-step methodology for building custom skills that make agents dramatically more productive. Whether you're coding with Claude Code or automating workflows with OpenClaw, this episode gives you the foundational knowledge to stop wasting tokens and start getting real results from your AI tools.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
00:42 – The Models Are Good Now
01:20 – How Context Windows Actually Work
04:55 – The Power of Skills
09:17 – How to create Skills
16:35 – Skill Maxxing
19:05 – What you need too build a project
20:40 – Recursively Building and Improving Skills
29:23 – Context Window Management and Token Efficiency
33:02 – Closing Thoughts
Key Points
* The models (Opus 4.6, GPT 5.4) are exceptionally good now — the differentiator is the context and harness you build around them.
* Agent md and claude md files get loaded into context on every single turn, burning tokens and degrading performance as the context window fills up. 95% of users can skip them entirely.
* Skills use progressive disclosure: only the name and description sit in context until the agent determines it needs the full file, saving thousands of tokens per conversation.
* The best way to create a skill is to walk through the workflow with the agent step by step, achieve a successful run, and then have the agent write the skill based on that real context.
* Recursively refine skills by feeding failures back into the agent and having it update the skill file so the same mistake is avoided going forward.
* Scale for productivity by starting with one agent and building up workflows before adding sub-agents — start simple, then expand.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Models Are Good — Context Is What Matters
Ras Mic opens by declaring that the current generation of models, Opus 4.6 and GPT 5.4, are exceptionally capable. The conversation is no longer about which model is "better" in a general sense. What matters now is the quality of context you feed them — that is what separates quality output from slop.
2. How Context Windows Work
Ras Mic walks through the anatomy of a context window: system prompt, agent.md files, skills, tools, the codebase, and the user conversation. All of these stack up as tokens, and the window has a hard limit (around 250,000 tokens). When you hit that limit, agents compact — and performance drops. Understanding this structure is the foundation for everything else in the episode.
3. Skills and Progressive Disclosure
Skills solve the token-bloat problem. A skill file contains a name, description, and the detailed instructions — but only the name and description are loaded into context. The agent reads the full file only when it determines the skill is relevant. This means a skill costs roughly 53 tokens per turn versus 944+ for an equivalent agent.md file.
4. Building Skills the Right Way
Ras Mic shares his methodology: identify a workflow, walk through it with the agent step by step, correct mistakes in real time, and only create the skill after you have completed a successful run. He illustrates this with his sponsor email screening agent — the first attempt returned all-positive results because the agent had no criteria for rejection.
5. Recursively Improving Skills
Even after a skill is created, the agent will still hit edge cases and fail. Ras Mic treats each failure as an opportunity: identify the error, have the agent fix it, then tell the agent to update the skill so the failure is documented. After five iterations of this loop on his YouTube analytics report generator, the agent now executes flawlessly across eight data sources in about ten minutes.
6. Scaling for Productivity Over Flash
Ras Mic started with a single agent handling everything — email, spreadsheets, research. Only after building reliable skills did he add sub-agents for marketing, business, and personal tasks. He argues that jumping straight to multi-agent architectures (or adopting tools like Paperclip without building foundational workflows first) optimizes for what looks cool rather than what is productive.

我和 Ras Mic 一起坐下来分析人工智能代理的实际工作原理以及为什么大多数人都错误地使用它们。 Ras Mic 解释了上下文窗口的机制，说明了代理 md 文件在很大程度上是不必要的，并分享了他构建自定义技能的分步方法，这些技能使代理显着提高了工作效率。无论您是使用 Claude Code 进行编码，还是使用 OpenClaw 实现工作流程自动化，本集都会为您提供基础知识，帮助您停止浪费代币并开始从 AI 工具中获得真正的结果。
时间戳
00:00 – 介绍
00:42 – 模型现在很好
01:20 – 上下文窗口实际上是如何工作的
04:55 – 技能的力量
09:17 – 如何创造技能
16:35 – 技能最大化
19:05 – 构建项目还需要什么
20:40 – 递归地建立和提高技能
29:23 – 上下文窗口管理和代币效率
33:02 – 结束语
要点
* 模型（Opus 4.6、GPT 5.4）现在非常好——区别在于您围绕它们构建的环境和工具。
* Agent md 和 claude md 文件在每一回合都会加载到上下文中，随着上下文窗口填满，会燃烧令牌并降低性能。 95% 的用户可以完全跳过它们。
* 技能使用渐进式披露：只有名称和描述位于上下文中，直到代理确定需要完整文件，从而在每次对话中节省数千个令牌。
* 创建技能的最佳方法是与代理一起逐步完成工作流程，实现成功运行，然后让代理根据真实上下文编写技能。
* 通过将失败反馈给代理并让它更新技能文件来递归地完善技能，从而避免今后出现同样的错误。
* 通过从一个代理开始并在添加子代理之前建立工作流程来扩展生产力 - 从简单开始，然后扩展。
编号部分摘要
1. 模型很好——背景才���最重要的
Ras Mic 首先宣称当前一代的型号 Opus 4.6 和 GPT 5.4 功能异常强大。讨论不再是一般意义上哪种模型“更好”。现在重要的是你为他们提供的上下文的质量——这就是高质量输出和废品的区别。
2. 上下文窗口如何工作
Ras Mic 详细剖析了上下文窗口：系统提示符、agent.md 文件、技能、工具、代码库和用户对话。所有这些都作为代币堆积起来，并且窗口有一个硬性限制（大约 250,000 个代币）。当达到该限制时，代理会紧凑，并且性能会下降。理解这个结构是本集中其他所有内容的基础。
3. 技能和渐进式披露
技能解决了令牌膨胀问题。技能文件包含名称、描述和详细说明，但只有名称和描述会加载到上下文中。仅当代理确定技能相关时才会读取完整��件。这意味着一项技能每回合大约花费 53 个代币，而同等的 agent.md 文件则需要 944+ 个代币。
4. 以正确的方式培养技能
Ras Mic 分享了他的方法论：确定工作流程，与代理一起逐步执行该工作流程，实时纠正错误，并且仅在成功完成运行后才创建技能。他用他的赞助商电子邮件筛选代理来说明这一点——第一次尝试返回了全阳性结果，因为代理没有拒绝标准。
5. 递归地提高技能
即使创建了技能后，代理仍然会遇到边缘情况并失败。 Ras Mic 将每次失败视为一次机会：识别错误，让代理修复它，然后告诉代理更新技能，以便记录失败。在 YouTube 分析报告生成器上循环执行五次之后，该代理现在可以在大约十分钟内完美地跨八个数据源执行。
6. 通过闪存扩展生产力
Ras Mic 最初由一个代理处理所有事务——电子邮件、电子表格、研究。只有在建立了可靠的技能之后，他才添加了负责营销、业务和个人任务的子代理。他认为，直接跳到多代理架构（或采用 Paperclip 等工具而不首先构建基础工作流程）会优化看起来很酷的东西，而不是高效的东西。
